[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list."}, {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "What is the class of this image?"}, {"type": "image_url", "url_kind": "data_uri", "length": 10231}]}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "<response>81</response>"}, {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "What is the class of this image?"}, {"type": "image_url", "url_kind": "data_uri", "length": 8831}]}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "<response>14</response>"}, {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Query Image:

Valid labels: [81, 14]

raw_response_text:
<features>
- observable feature: flower has large, broad petals forming a star-like shape
- observable feature: petals are a solid, deep purple color
- observable feature: center of flower has a prominent, fuzzy, light-colored structure
</features>
<kb>
- IF [flower has large, broad petals forming a star-like shape AND petals are a solid, deep purple color AND center has a prominent, fuzzy, light-colored structure] THEN [81]
- IF [flower has three upright petals and three drooping petals AND petals are bright yellow AND center has a distinct pattern] THEN [14]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- Rule for class 81 fires because the Query Image has large, broad petals forming a star-like shape, solid deep purple petals, and a prominent fuzzy light-colored center, matching the features described in the rule for class 81.
</rule_check>
<response>81</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- observable feature: flower color is yellow
- observable feature: flower has three upright petals and three drooping petals
- observable feature: flower has a distinct central structure with yellow markings
</features>
<kb>
- IF [flower color is yellow AND flower has three upright petals and three drooping petals] THEN [14]
- IF [flower has pink and white stripes AND multiple petals radiating from center] THEN [81]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- Rule 1 fires because the Query Image shows a yellow flower with the characteristic three upright and three drooping petal structure, matching the definition for class 14.
- Rule 2 does not fire because the flower is not pink and white striped or radiating from a center.
</rule_check>
<response>14</response>

- observable feature: flower has three upright petals and three drooping petals
- observable feature: flower has a distinct central structure with yellow markings
</features>
<kb>
